title: Direct and dual Information Bottleneck frameworks for Deep Learning
speaker: Tali Tishby
abstract: The Information Bottleneck (IB) is an information theoretic framework for optimal representation learning. It stems from the problem of finding minimal sufficient statistics in supervised learning, but has insightful implications for Deep Learning. In particular, its the only theory that gives concrete predictions on the different representations in each layer and their potential computational benefit.

proof_stages.label: Generalization paradox of overparameterized deep networks
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates its problem by contrasting classical sample-complexity rules with the empirical success of deep networks whose number of parameters vastly exceeds the number of training examples.

proof_stages.label: Information-flow formalism for network layers
proof_stages.summary: The speaker replaces worst-case learning bounds by typical information-theoretic quantities, views layers as a Markov chain of representations, and introduces KL divergence, mutual information, data processing, and variational estimation of information.

proof_stages.label: Two-phase information-plane dynamics
proof_stages.summary: Empirical information-plane trajectories show a fast fitting phase followed by a slow compression phase in which representations forget input information while retaining label information, with insufficient data causing over-compression.

proof_stages.label: Information coordinates as order and complexity parameters
proof_stages.summary: Local factorization assumptions explain concentration of the mutual-information coordinates, while a counting argument shows that encoder information controls effective function-class size and hence sample complexity.

proof_stages.label: Gradient signal-to-noise transition
proof_stages.summary: The onset of compression is tied to the point where training error saturates and gradients pass from high-SNR drift to low-SNR fluctuation-dominated diffusion.

proof_stages.label: Information Bottleneck frontier and finite-sample pressure
proof_stages.summary: The Information Bottleneck variational problem gives an architecture-independent optimal frontier for representations, and finite-sample deviations push the best attainable representation leftward toward compression.

proof_stages.label: Diffusive SGD mechanism and depth-scaling prediction
proof_stages.summary: Modeling low-SNR SGD as diffusion in irrelevant directions and bounding interlayer information by a noisy-channel calculation yields power-law predictions for convergence time versus depth, including an optimal finite depth.

proof_stages.label: Critical slowing down and layer locations
proof_stages.summary: Cusps and singularities along the Information Bottleneck curve act as critical points where convergence slows, explaining why layers settle at reproducible representation levels.

examples.name: Noisy regression and over-flexible curve fitting
examples.note: Noisy data points, high-degree polynomials, high-frequency sinusoids, and the two-points lab warning make the classical overfitting/generalization problem concrete.

examples.name: Canyon of good solutions
examples.note: The optimization-landscape canyon illustrates why noisy gradients diffuse mostly in irrelevant directions after fitting has saturated.
examples.engagement_reason: he develops the canyon picture by analyzing Hessian directions, noise-driven diffusion, and its effect on compression and convergence

passes.reason: flat manifold of low-error solutions is minimizing and Hessian-degenerate in many directions
The canyon of good solutions is a flat manifold of near-minimizers with many Hessian-null or irrelevant directions.

The canyon is a low-error solution set with many flat Hessian directions, hence a minimizing but degenerate valley.
